body:
Subject: Storage / Pricing Org Structure

Drew,

I'm reviewing the reporting structure for our commercial groups and want your input on a specific question.

We're evaluating whether to keep Storage and Pricing as separate teams or merge them under a single lead. The upside is a cleaner line into the commercial book. The counter-argument is that Storage carries regulatory complexity that may warrant independent management oversight.

From your vantage point, does the merger create any compliance or regulatory exposure we don't have today? Any language in our FERC obligations, tariff responsibilities, or contract structures that would be harder to manage without a dedicated Storage head?

I need to make a recommendation in the next few weeks. Your take on the legal/regulatory footprint will factor heavily.

Call me if you'd rather talk it through.

Dan

Dan,

Good question. Here's my take on the regulatory footprint.

From a FERC compliance standpoint, I don't see the merger creating new exposure per se. The tariff obligations and capacity responsibilities attach to the assets and contracts themselves, not to org structure. Storage's regulatory functions—rate design, tariff filings, capacity release compliance—can technically be performed under either structure.

That said, there's a practical risk worth flagging. Storage operations require a certain level of specialized oversight. If the combined lead is spread thin across both groups, the regulatory detail gets diluted. We've built some of our compliance rigor around having a dedicated Storage manager who knows the FERC docket and capacity release rules cold. Lose that and you increase the chance of filings slipping or contract language getting misapplied.

My recommendation: the merger works if—and only if—the combined lead has either strong Storage regulatory experience or can retain a dedicated subject matter expert reporting into that role. Don't assume the legal/regulatory work scales down just because the org flattens.

If you want, I can pull together a one-pager on the specific FERC obligations we'd need to ensure are covered. Let me know.

df

Drew,

That's the kind of specificity I needed. The practical oversight point is the real consideration here—not the legal exposure itself, but what happens to the detail when the structure flattens.

Go ahead and pull that one-pager together. Focus on the FERC docket obligations and capacity release compliance—those are the two areas where I could see things slipping if we don't have the right expertise in the room.

One thing I'm going to build into the recommendation regardless: the combined lead will need a designated Storage SME reporting directly. Your point about not assuming the regulatory work scales down is well taken.

I'll loop you in when I have a draft structure to review. Thanks for the quick turnaround.

Dan
Will do. I'll have the one-pager to you by end of next week. Looking forward to reviewing the draft structure when you're ready.
